speaker: interviewer
text: 1995. Now we're reaching the DP camp in Germany, in Pocking.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to-- we were arrested. In between, we spent a few weeks-- something-- in Czechoslovakia-- arrested. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get arrested? How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a illegal transport to Germany. So we were told we should-- if somebody talked to us, we should answer in Hebrew. And we were supposed to be Greeks. So we came to the border of Czechoslovakia, and we were detained. We were arrested. And we were taken to a empty room-- a closed up restaurant. And we were placed there-- no beds, no other-- we slept on the tables and the-- on the floors-- [LAUGHS] --on the floor. And we were detained there. Eventually, we were transferred to another place which had bunk beds already. And we were try-- Joint tried to intervene. And we were taken out there and allowed to go to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this in Czechoslovakia that you were staying? Do you know? Do you happen to know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't remember at this point.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got to Pocking after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It could have been Morava-- Morava or [NON-ENGLISH] something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we got to Pocking. And there there were these long barracks, you know. And we stayed there. And-- and it was like far away from everything. There was no offices of any-- and we were told that in Foehrenwald there was a HIAS office, and there was from the Joint Distribution office. And I wanted to get to registered to America. So I was advised that if I go to Foehrenwald, I would take care of that. So I traveled myself to Foehrenwald. I left my mother there. And I had, in Foehrenwald, friends that I knew-- a landslide. And they arranged for me to get a room. And I moved to Foehrenwald. I registered to-- with the HIAS. And I was waiting my next to go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there my mother got very sick. She started to-- to suffer, really. She had high blood pressure. She was in and out of the hospital. And she was in Munich, in the hospital, for a while. And in 1947 my mother had a stroke. And this was a very hard time after the war, you know. And like I said, she was in and out of the hospital, and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, eventually, they-- they sent my mother to a-- not a convalescent home. She was in the convalescent home also, near my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sanitarium?

speaker: interviewee
text: --near about-- various-- often, she was in a convalescent home for a few months. But then they send her to a like a [NON-ENGLISH]-- a chronic hospital, and that was in Bad Worishofen. But somehow, the-- there was-- they did-- there was nobody to pay for that. So I tried to get the mayor's office. And I struggled until I think I-- I got to the mayor's secretary. And the mayor OK to pay for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we traveled to Bad Worishofen, and there we came in '48, I believe. There I met my husband. So I stayed with my mother in the same room. I had some problems too, you know-- health problems. So I was allowed to stay with my mother. And there I met my husband. And we got engaged. Was he sick, or was he in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was working there. He was-- as a policeman, part-time. And part-time he was the [NON-ENGLISH]. He was working in the kitchen. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] There I attended, again, some dress making. There was a class that they taught how to make underwear, I believe-- men's underwear. [LAUGHTER] But just I wanted to learn-- to brush up on sewing. So-- and there is-- there was nothing. I couldn't work there. I couldn't get a job. So I was doing nothing. So I was attending that class. And we-- then we got engaged. And I got married in the-- the courthouse. I took out marriage license. And people, the doctors, they congratulate me that I'm married. And I said, no! I'm not married. [LAUGHS] Because according to the Jewish law, I wasn't married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I wanted to get married when I come to United States. Because my mother was able to travel to United States with-- by plane, because she was very sick. She couldn't go by boat. So my mother went first, and I stayed behind to go to wait for the ship. So-- so I-- I stayed behind. And then my mother-- and I was supposed to come to America and get married in America. So my mother was here with her brothers. And she told them that story that I'm engaged. She was very happy. So my mother wrote me letters that it's-- she wrote that in America, it's very expensive to get married.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] She overheard the discussions that-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --you know, to make a wedding. And-- and she didn't want to fall-- how should I say it? Burden. Burden.

speaker: interviewee
text: Burden the family, that's right. So she wrote me I should get married. So I went to a rabbi, and I got married. I was very upset that my mother couldn't be at my wedding. [LAUGHS] But we were so naive. I could have gotten married here at the rabbi's office. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I-- I came-- I came by ship. What ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to America. General Haan.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Haan?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-A-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: H-A-A-N.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, I had like a-- a little magazine that was written on the ship, in Polish and in English. So I gave it to the Living Memorial Museum. They have it. And I gave to them also some paintings-- not paintings-- photographs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Photographs.

speaker: interviewee
text: I gave photographs for them-- some documents.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive on the ship in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: To New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: To New York, yes. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't talk about the DP camp, which was nothing-- nothing special. It was a self-- how should I say? Foehrenwald Camp-- I don't have to describe that. Do it. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came here to America, and I-- my uncle invited me as a guest for a week. There, I stayed. And he wanted me to live on the farm. He wanted to rent the farm, or maybe even buy for me a farm-- something. And I didn't want to live in a-- in a village. I said, I had enough village life.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Where did he live?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lived in a-- on a farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: In New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Connecticut-- Colchester, Connecticut. So I refused to live on a farm. And I-- the family ran and got me a furnished room. So I lived in that one room-- furnished room-- in a cousin of mine. His name is Victor [NON-ENGLISH]-- had a large apartment, so I lived in a furnished room for, like, a year or so until we got an apartment. And after a year, I-- my daughter was born. My daughter Lillian was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born on December 24, 1950. So that first year, I worked. In fact, I traveled from the Bronx to Brooklyn-- a job that I made $30 a week. [LAUGHS] And then I took care of my daughter. While my daughter was growing up, I started painting. I always had the urge for art. In fact, in the DP camp, I used to do it a little. Some people attended schools from the DP camps. But I was so busy with taking care of my mother, so I could not enter any school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when my daughter was growing, I registered with a night school-- evening courses-- adult education. And I attended art-- art classes. And eventually, I started painting in the house. And in the '70s I became more serious.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- in fact, when I moved here in 1970, I moved to this development. And I got to know a Chinese man. He was a very good teacher. So I organized a art class here, and he used to come here once a week and give lessons. So I, at that time, I started painting more seriously. And after painting for a while, I didn't realize that I'm making, like a document. And after a while, I realized that I'm, you know, painting very important, you know, memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I joined-- I was taking a course with Morris Katz. He's a well-known artist. Now he became a schlock artist-- they call him.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] And he encouraged me to join into a exhibit, a outdoor exhibit. And at the exhibit, I-- my work got a lot of attention. In fact, there were professional people exhibiting there. And people would pass by their work, which was very professional, and they stopped to look at my work. And I had this woman washing clothing, and a woman working in a-- with a hand mill, and so forth. I painted my memories from my childhood. And that sort of encouraged me to continue painting that subject. And I-- later I couldn't get away from this subject.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember some people told me, why don't you paint flowers or scenery? I did make a few sceneries to begin with. But then I-- I was compelled to paint my memories from childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I had enough paintings that I felt I have a story to tell. And my daughter, Lillian, got in touch with some publishers to ask if I could publish this. And, of course, they said I don't have a chance. Unless I'm a well-known artist, I have no chance of publishing a book. But they suggested if I write a narration, maybe I have a better chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I just started writing.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I'm the type that I'll try anything, you know? So I started writing, and I struggled. I had a hard time. Eventually, some people that I shared the photographs of my paintings-- I showed them-- called me if I would join in a discussion about a shtetl. And I-- and I came, and I showed them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I wouldn't go, because I felt I couldn't speak in front of the public, and so forth. And I showed them the slides, and I felt maybe they will use the slides. So, in turn, they developed my slides into a slide presentation. And the rabbi read the-- my narration that I wrote. And-- and people enjoyed that very much. And that's encouraged me to continue with the slide presentation-- which I did. Meantime, my daughter sent-- my daughter got in touch with Eva. And Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] wrote a book about Poland, at the time. And they were trying to make a film about the same subject. So they came, and they interviewed me for that film. So they included nine of my paintings there. And eventually, I got to a-- to a agent by chance. And the agent sold my book. And Alfred Knopf published my book, Now my daughter, in 1977-- my daughter got married. In fact, that was her way to reciprocate for the wedding-- that she got in touch with the [NON-ENGLISH] to do something about my painting. Because I was so anxious to publish-- [LAUGHS] --my painting. My book came out in 1990. And the-- from the children's department-- the-- the people from the children's department asked me to write a-- a children's book. And-- and this I wrote--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your-- you have a son-in-law. His name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Steven-- Steven Finkler. He has--

speaker: interviewer
text: And grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: --and two grandchildren, David and Gary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are attending yeshiva, [NON-ENGLISH] Yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, your husband's full name-- I don't know if we--

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband is-- [LAUGHS] --Chaim-Mendel. And I neglected to-- to tell how I met my family here and the reactions. Should I?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. We have just a couple of minutes. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came here, the family somehow was very anxious. They-- they felt they will have to support us, and they were afraid that we will, you know, be living with them, and so forth. But we were very independent. And we right away went to work. My husband went to work. And I went to work. And we didn't ask for any help from the family, except moral support. And then we got along well with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Let's sum this up, because we want to do some of your photographs and paintings.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I want to just to sum it up with one question. Why did you decide to do this tape at this moment in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel that this is a very good idea. In fact, I want to commend Spielberg, Mr. Spielberg, that he does that-- that the-- the following generation should know what was happening. And it shouldn't be forgotten. And so many people write that, unfortunately, that it never happened. That's a lie. So I feel that I will contribute. For the future generations should learn what was going on during the Hitler era.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken in 1906 or 1907, in [NON-ENGLISH]. Starting from left, seated is my great grandmother-- which I don't know her name. Next to her, the little girl, is my Aunt Sarah Smoller. Standing is my mother, [NON-ENGLISH]. Next to my mother is my grandmother, [NON-ENGLISH] Ruchl. Her name is Ruchl Schwartz. Next to her, the little boy is Sam, or [NON-ENGLISH]. Above him-- in back of him, rather-- is Joseph. And to-- next to him, to the right, is my grandfather, Schloime Schwartz. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Czernica, in 1930. On my-- on left-- at left, this is myself. And on the right is my brother, Aron.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said at this time you were called [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time I was called [NON-ENGLISH], yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in-- taken in Czernica, in 1928. At left is my sister, Surcie. On the right is my mother, [NON-ENGLISH]. The original picture I gave to the Living Memorial Museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: In New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York. On the left is my mother. On the right is myself. That's the first picture after-- soon after liberation, when we were in the DP camp, in Foehrenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were already in Germany 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '45. And that-- I think that's her. This was taken in Bad Nauheim. That was a sanitarium for-- for heart disease patients. On the left is myself. On the right is my mother. And there is a nurse standing on the right. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Foehrenwald, in 1947, at a meeting at Betar, a Zionist organization. I am on the left. Next to me, to my right, is my best friend. And the other girls are friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were the first on the left-- just to make sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: First on the left. This was taken in Bad Woerishofen at a-- a school for dressmaking. I am on the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beginning of 1949. This is my wedding picture. It was taken in Bad Woerishofen, in 1949. I got married in Landsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in New York, in 1993. The-- the little boy with glasses is my grandson, Gary.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left. In back of him is his father, my son-in-law, Steve-- Steven Finkler. To his right is Lillian, my daughter. To Lillian's right is David, my grandson. Seated below is myself. And left to myself is my husband. My daughter wanted to be with me, but she had difficulty coming. She was sorry that she couldn't be with me. David and Gary-- you read my book. I was telling you stories of my survival during the Nazis. And I want you to tell your children to carry on. I'm leaving the legacy to you that you will carry on to tell the next generations to come. It shouldn't be forgotten-- the horror of the Nazis. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You will-- --this is a painting of my-- my farm. You will find it in my book,

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is Czernica, Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czernica, Poland. And this is myself coming from school, and my father sawing the wood, and my mother in the background feeding the chickens. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a painting of my mother kneading bread in Czernica, on the farm. And you will find that painting in my book, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [LAUGHS] You don't want me to--

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture--

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of red potatoes. I remember myself eating potatoes all the time. And I wanted to paint this picture. You'll find it in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This painting is of making [NON-ENGLISH]. I have the nuts, the apples, and the wine. In the mortar and pestle we crush the nuts. And this you find in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the memory of the Seder at home. As you see, my father is lying on the bed, leaning on the pillows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is the custom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which is the custom in that region. And it's from the same book. This is my father. I don't have a photograph of him, because he never allowed to take a photograph according to the Jewish law. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my mother, [NON-ENGLISH]. She always wore a scarf, because she was orthodox. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my oldest sister, Surcie. She really saved my life. Because of her, I ran away from the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is myself. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: At left? On the left is my sister, Lajcie. On the right is my brother, Aron.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't have a photograph of my wedding, and this inspired me to do the drawing. It was in Foehrenwald. Genia Praver was the [NON-ENGLISH] here because my mother wasn't with me. I want to end with this painting-- with this drawing. This is the-- the life I started with my husband and built a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Thank you. Thank you for sharing your life with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was really very rewarding to-- to talk about it. And I feel I'm leaving a legacy for the generations to come-- for my grandchildren. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
